steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Ehmann's hypothesis: because males disperse and females stay, male offspring (individual 17) should share a smaller portion of their mother's dietary preferences than female offspring (individual 5). Supporting data must show both a smaller mother-offspring diet overlap for the male and a larger unshared portion of the male's diet — two metrics, both pointing the same direction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The majority of the diet of the mother of individual 5 was shared... whereas significantly less than half the diet of the mother of individual 17 was shared...': This only reports one metric (percent of the mother's diet shared) and says nothing about the percent of the individual's own diet that is unshared. A single metric is not sufficient to support both parts of the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The percent of the mother\'s diet shared with its offspring\'s diet was smaller for individual 17 than for individual 5, and the percent of the individual\'s diet not shared with its mother\'s diet was greater for individual 17 than for individual 5': This captures both measurements from the graph and shows that individual 17 (male) diverges from its mother more than individual 5 (female) does on both measures — exactly what the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Neither individual 17 nor individual 5 had a diet that consisted mostly of food not shared... whereas more than half of both individuals\' mothers\' diets were shared...': This misstates the graph. The graph shows that individual 17's mother shared only roughly 10% of her diet with individual 17 — well below "more than half." ✗
  • Choice D — 'The percent of individual 5\'s mother\'s diet that was shared... ranged from approximately 59% to approximately 65%, whereas the percent of individual 17\'s mother\'s diet that was shared... ranged from approximately 8% to approximately 19%': This reports only one metric (percent of the mother's diet shared) with no data on the percent of the individual's own diet that is unshared. One metric alone does not fully support the hypothesis. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
